bar.name: La Cuevita
bar.address: 5922 N Figueroa St, Los Angeles, CA 90042
bar.happyHour: Tequila and mezcal focused, agave spirit cocktails
bar.vibe: Mexican grotto bar, Dia de los Muertos decor, exposed brick, wood beams, stained glass, 100-year-old church doors from Mexico, leather stools, salvaged marble tables, dim candlelit cave atmosphere
barFacts: Formerly a dive bar called Little Cave — one of the first bars to open in Highland Park in decades (2003). Transformed by 1933 Group into La Cuevita on May 4, 2012
During renovation, stripping stucco/plaster/paneling revealed the building's early 1930s bones — exposed brick walls and original structure
Entrance features 100-year-old doors salvaged from a church in Mexico
1933 Group also operates Highland Park Bowl (3 blocks south) — both are Prohibition-era-adjacent Highland Park restorations
Interior decorated with skulls, skeletons, upside-down bats, reclaimed wood, stained glass lighting, wrought iron fixtures

pois.name: Chicken Boy
facts: 22-foot-tall fiberglass statue — man's body with a chicken head, holding a bucket of chicken
Originally built in the 1960s for the Chicken Boy Fried Chicken Restaurant on Broadway in downtown LA, near Grand Central Market
Customized Muffler Man statue from International Fiberglass Company in Venice, CA — fiberglass and polyester resin on a steel frame
Restaurant owner died in 1984. Art director Amy Inouye rescued the statue and stored it for 23 years
Installed atop Future Studios at 5558 N Figueroa in 2007, funded partly by a grant from the Office of City Beautification
Governor Schwarzenegger granted it a Governor's Historic Preservation Award in 2010
Called 'the Statue of Liberty of Los Angeles' by locals
Inspired a tile mosaic by Wick Alexander in the Figueroa Street sidewalk (installed 2018)

features.detail: 22-foot fiberglass statue: man's body, chicken head, holding bucket
features.material: fiberglass and polyester resin on steel frame

features.detail: Visible from blocks away — towers over the street from building rooftop
Sidewalk tile mosaic of Chicken Boy by Wick Alexander

features.text: Future Studios signage on building facade

connections.link: Statue traveled from Broadway to 23 years of storage to Figueroa Street — the neighborhood adopted a downtown castoff
Both are midcentury commercial Americana repurposed as cultural landmarks
Chicken Boy inspired one of the 14 Wick Alexander mosaics in the sidewalk below

pois.name: Highland Theatre
facts: Opened March 5, 1925. First film: 'The Lady of the Night' starring Norma Shearer, who attended the premiere in person

Designed by theater architect Lewis A. Smith in Moorish Revival style — Smith also designed the Vista Theatre (Los Feliz), El Portal (North Hollywood), Rialto (Pasadena). He died in 1926, just a year after Highland opened
Originally 1,450-seat single-screen palace. Later converted to triplex
Part of a 4-block Figueroa theater district in the 1920s-30s
Closed in spring 2024, six days short of its 100th anniversary — business never recovered post-pandemic
Purchased by Kristen Stewart in February 2026, who plans to reopen it as a cultural center: 'We want to make it a family affair, something for the community'
Stewart's vision: screenings, events, community programs — restoring original architectural elements while creating a gathering place

features.detail: Moorish Revival three-story facade on Figueroa
Ornate Moorish-style arches and decorative elements visible from street
Three-story building dominates the block

connections.link: Built within two years of each other — Highland Park's 1920s entertainment boom on Figueroa
Three-block cluster of 1920s institutional buildings on Figueroa
Smith designed a constellation of LA theaters but died at ~30, one year after Highland opened
Stewart purchased 2026 — Hollywood actress buying the neighborhood's original movie palace

pois.name: Highland Park Bowl
designations: National Trust for Historic Preservation feature
facts: Opened 1927 during Prohibition — oldest operating bowling alley in Los Angeles
Prohibition business model: doctors' offices upstairs issued prescriptions for 'medicinal whiskey', filled at the Highland Park Drug Company pharmacy downstairs, consumed while bowling
Building housed 8 bowling lanes, doctors' offices, a pharmacy, and a music store
Renamed Mr. T's Bowl in 1966 for new owner Joseph 'Mr. T' Theresa
Became a prominent punk rock venue in the early 1990s — black curtain blocked off lanes for performances. The Breeders and Germs played here
Closed 2014. 1933 Group (same operators as La Cuevita) acquired and restored the Spanish Revival facade, original lanes, and ball-return machinery
Reopened 2016 with Roaring Twenties aesthetic — the same company runs both La Cuevita and Highland Park Bowl

features.detail: Spanish Revival facade, restored by 1933 Group
Multi-story building — bowling below, former offices above
8 original Brunswick bowling lanes visible through windows/entry

connections.link: Same bar group operates both — 3 blocks apart on Figueroa
Both 1920s Figueroa entertainment institutions
Both buildings found ways to serve booze through unconventional means

pois.name: Highland Park Masonic Temple / Lodge Room
facts: Three-story brick building completed 1923 for Lodge 382, Free and Accepted Masons
Ground floor: retail shops. Second floor: lodge hall and banquet room
Lodge Room has 18-foot ceiling, cherry wood paneling, original embossed cotton anaglypta wall coverings
Paintings at both ends of the hall depict Egyptian scenes — sphinx, pyramid
Masons vacated 1983 — couldn't afford seismic retrofitting costs
Reopened 1988 as Highland Hall Cultural Center (weddings, quinceañeras, film shoots)
Converted to 500-capacity music venue 'Lodge Room' in 2017 — original cherry wood paneling and Masonic details preserved
Corner of Avenue 56 and North Figueroa

features.detail: Three-story brick building on corner of Ave 56 and Figueroa
Masonic architectural details on exterior

connections.link: Both anchor the same Figueroa corridor, built 2 years apart
Both buildings: original purpose died, building sat dormant, revived as entertainment venue
Same intersection — Ave 56 & Figueroa

pois.name: Figueroa Street Sidewalk Mosaics
pois.address: N Figueroa St between Ave 53 and Ave 60
designations: City of LA Dept of Transportation public art commission
facts: 14 tile mosaics depicting Highland Park landmarks and history, embedded in Figueroa Street sidewalks between Avenues 53 and 60
Created by tile artist Wick Alexander, based on photos of local landmarks and people
Funded by a $149,000 grant from the LA City Department of Transportation
Subjects include: Chicken Boy, Highland Theatre with trolley cars, women of the Ebell Club in front of their 1912 clubhouse, street name markers
Mosaics depict both past (trolley era) and present (current landmarks)
Most pedestrians walk over them without noticing — you have to look down

features.detail: Tile mosaics embedded flush in sidewalk

features.element: Total mosaics between Ave 53 and Ave 60

features.detail: Chicken Boy mosaic near 5558 N Figueroa
Highland Theatre with trolley car mosaic
Ebell Club women mosaic
Flush with sidewalk — easy to miss unless looking down

connections.link: Chicken Boy mosaic is embedded in the sidewalk beneath the actual statue
One mosaic depicts the theater with trolley cars — showing what Figueroa looked like in the 1920s

connections.from: Sidewalk mosaics (you have to look down)
connections.to: Chicken Boy (you have to look up)
connections.link: Same block: the art is above and below — most people miss both

pois.name: Mexico-Tenochtitlan: The Wall That Talks
pois.address: N Figueroa St at N Avenue 61
designations: Nominated for LA Historic-Cultural Monument status (2022)
facts: 12-by-75-foot mural on the corner of N Figueroa and N Avenue 61
Painted in 1996 by the Quetzalcoatl Mural Project art collective, founded by Anthony 'Eagle' Ortega
Created after the loss of a friend to gang violence — the mural was a response to that grief
Celebrates Chicano culture by blending Aztec, Mayan, Native American, African and other cultural iconography representing indigenous cultures in unity
Threatened with erasure in 2020 — building owner denied plans but the rumor sparked community mobilization
Nominated for historic landmark status in 2022 to permanently protect it
One of the most significant Chicano murals in Northeast LA

features.detail: 12×75 ft mural spanning building facade
Bold colors — Aztec, Mayan, and indigenous figures and symbols
Portraiture blended with cultural symbolism and natural imagery
Closest POI to La Cuevita — 110m, visible from the bar's block

connections.to: La Cuevita (Dia de los Muertos decor)
connections.link: Both celebrate Mexican and indigenous cultural identity on the same stretch of Figueroa

connections.to: Judy Baca's History of Highland Park (1977)
connections.link: Two generations of Chicano murals in the same neighborhood — both community-created responses to neighborhood trauma
110m — the mural is practically the bar's front yard

pois.name: Highland Park Ebell Club
facts: Women's club founded December 1903 at the Highland Park home of Ella G. Lunt
Named for Dr. Adrian Ebell, 19th-century pioneer in women's education who traveled from Oakland organizing 'circles for the study of arts and science'
Local women raised the money, bought the land, and financed construction themselves
Designed by Sumner Hunt and Silas Burns — same architects as the Southwest Museum
Completed 1912. Style: eclectic blend of Italianate, Mission Revival, and Prairie elements
Members awarded scholarships starting 1907 and organized for women's suffrage — California granted women the vote in 1911, 9 years before the 19th Amendment
One of the largest women's clubs in the nation in the early 1900s, rivaled only by the Friday Morning Club in downtown LA
Depicted in one of Wick Alexander's sidewalk mosaics on Figueroa

features.detail: Italianate/Mission Revival/Prairie style clubhouse
Distinctive enough to be subject of a sidewalk mosaic

connections.link: Same architects designed both — the women's club and the museum, both in Highland Park
California women got the vote in 1911, partly organized from this building
Ebell women depicted in one of the 14 Wick Alexander mosaics

pois.name: Temple Beth Israel of Highland Park and Eagle Rock
facts: Oldest synagogue in Los Angeles exclusively operating in its original location
Organized as the Highland Park Hebrew School Association in 1923. Building completed 1930 at a cost of $4,078 (today ~$79,000)
Spanish Colonial Revival style, designed by USC student Gerson C. Cohen, built by carpenter Abe Goldberg and congregants
Sanctuary later remodeled by Jerome Share in a mid-century Art Deco style
In July 2023, Rabbi Alex Weisz became the first Gen Z rabbi serving as spiritual leader of an American synagogue
Conservative Jewish congregation — still active

features.detail: Spanish Colonial Revival exterior
Small-scale synagogue building on Monte Vista St

connections.link: Both late-1920s Highland Park institutions — the neighborhood's diverse roots

connections.from: Temple Beth Israel (oldest synagogue in original location)
connections.link: The synagogue survived because Highland Park preserved what other LA neighborhoods demolished

pois.name: Pisgah Home Historic District
facts: Site of the Pisgah Home movement founded by Finis E. Yoakum, a doctor who became a faith healer after a near-fatal buggy accident in 1894
Yoakum specialized in mental and neurological disorders at Gross Medical College in Denver before moving to LA in 1895
Started ~1900 as 'Yoakum's Sanatorium' — 8-bed home 'to give free care to drunkards and outcasts who wished to reform'
By 1903 the LA Times called Yoakum the 'leading apostle of the faith-healing movement in this city', claiming 800+ healings
Named 'Pisgah' after the biblical mountain from which Moses first saw the promised land
During the Azusa Street revival (founding of Pentecostal Church), Yoakum hosted followers at the Highland Park mission site
Closely aligned with the founding of the modern Pentecostal church
Today operated as Christ Faith Mission / Old Pisgah Home

features.detail: Historic district with early 1900s structures

connections.link: Highland Park attracted reformers, healers, and religious movements from its founding
Both from Highland Park's first generation — before the 1920s building boom

pois.name: Smith Estate / El Mio
facts: Victorian Queen Anne hilltop mansion built 1887, designed by Abram M. Edelman
Named 'El Mio' (Spanish: 'mine' or 'my place') by the Smith family — the street is named after the house
Residents include: a judge who wrote books on occultism, the head of the Los Angeles Railway, a deputy mayor
Filming location for cult films: Spider Baby (1964), Silent Scream, Insidious: Chapter 2
One of the oldest surviving Victorian homes in Highland Park

features.detail: Queen Anne Victorian mansion on hilltop
Perched on hilltop — dramatic visibility from below

connections.link: The person who ran LA's trolley system lived on a hill overlooking Highland Park — the trolleys that ran past the Highland Theatre

connections.to: La Cuevita (Day of the Dead bar)
connections.link: Highland Park has a strain of the macabre running through its history — horror films, Dia de los Muertos, grotto bars

pois.routeNote: Outside comfortable walking radius — supplemental POI, not a route stop

pois.name: Southwest Museum of the American Indian
facts: Founded by Charles Lummis in 1907 — LA's first museum. Opened to the public 1914
Lummis walked from Ohio to Los Angeles in 1884, documenting the journey as a journalist
He founded the Landmarks Club in 1895 to restore California's deteriorating 18th-century missions — considered the father of historic preservation in Southern California
Designed by Sumner Hunt & Silas Burns (same architects as the Ebell Club) in Mission Revival style
Housed one of the world's finest collections of Native American artifacts
Collections transferred to Autry Museum in Griffith Park after 2003 merger — building largely closed to public
Lummis also built his own home, El Alisal, by hand from arroyo stones over 12 years (completed 1910), a few blocks away

features.detail: Mission Revival building on hilltop above Arroyo Seco
Dramatic hilltop position visible from distance

connections.link: Lummis built the museum and his own stone house by hand — both within a mile of each other
Same architects designed both — Highland Park's two civic anchors
Lummis's preservation ethic became the neighborhood's identity — LA's largest Historic Preservation Overlay Zone

pois.routeNote: Outside 500m walk radius — contextual POI, enriches neighborhood narrative

pois.name: Lummis House / El Alisal
facts: Hand-built stone house constructed by Charles Lummis over 12 years (1898-1910) using boulders from the Arroyo Seco
'El Alisal' means 'alder grove' in Spanish
Lummis trained a few Native American laborers in carpentry — they assisted, but the stones were gathered and placed by hand
Built during the Arts and Crafts movement in Southern California — the hand-hewn character was deliberate, ideological
Now a city-run house museum with a garden of native plants
Historical Society of Southern California occupied it for 50 years until 2015

features.detail: Rustic Craftsman stone house on edge of Arroyo Seco
Entirely hand-built from river boulders — visible in every wall

pois.routeNote: 1.5km from bar — background POI for Highland Park narrative, not a route stop

pois.name: Heritage Square Museum
facts: Open-air architecture museum of 8 Victorian-era buildings saved from demolition during LA's 1960s expansion
Created by the Cultural Heritage Foundation in 1969 at the request of the LA Cultural Heritage Commission
Buildings include Hale House (HCM #40, declared 1966 — saved when a gas station was planned for the site)
Structures were physically relocated to Heritage Square as a 'last-chance haven'
Hale House relocated in 1970 — one of the first buildings saved by the LA historic monument program

features.detail: Cluster of Victorian houses from different LA neighborhoods

pois.name: History of Highland Park (Judy Baca mural)
facts: 125-foot-long mural by Judy Baca (1977-78) on two walls of the AT&T building at 1207 N Avenue 56
Baca is the artist behind The Great Wall of Los Angeles in the San Fernando Valley — the longest mural in the world
Restored in 2011 with a $78,000 project led by Baca, funded by AT&T, SPARC, and Councilman Jose Huizar
Part of Baca's citywide program of community murals

features.detail: 125-ft mural spanning two walls of AT&T building
On a telephone company building — corporate infrastructure as art canvas

connections.link: Same artist painted the longest mural in the world — this Highland Park mural is from the same era
Two generations of Chicano muralism on the same streets — Baca's generation and Ortega's generation

stop1_chickenBoy.notes: Chicken Boy is a spotter home run — 22ft fiberglass statue you have to look UP to see, then 14 sidewalk mosaics you have to look DOWN to see, in the same block. Reframe: 'It was an ad for a chicken restaurant before it was a landmark. It lived in storage for 23 years.' Sequence: the mosaics form a numbered set. Narrative riddle: Amy Inouye's 23-year rescue story. Masonic Temple/Lodge Room adds the Egyptian paintings and cherry wood paneling for supplemental spotter material.

stop2_highlandTheatre.notes: Highland Theatre is the strongest single POI. Narrative riddle: 'An architect designed theaters across LA but died at ~30, a year after this one opened. 100 years later, a Twilight actress bought it.' Reframe: 'It closed 6 days before its 100th birthday.' Connector: links to Highland Park Bowl (same era, same street, same Prohibition-era ecosystem). Cipher-lite: 'The Lady of the Night' (first film) or Lewis A. Smith. HP Bowl's Prohibition prescription scheme is a devastating narrative riddle on its own. Ebell Club adds suffrage and the Hunt/Burns architect connection.

stop3_tenochtitlanWall.notes: The Wall That Talks is a 75-foot mural with identifiable Aztec/Mayan figures — rich photo-challenge and spotter territory. Narrative riddle: 'It was painted after a friend was killed by gang violence — the artists turned grief into the most important Chicano mural in Northeast LA.' Connector to La Cuevita: both celebrate Mexican cultural identity on the same stretch of Figueroa. Temple Beth Israel and Pisgah Home add depth — oldest synagogue in its original location, Pentecostal founding site. Reframe: the mural was nearly erased in 2020.

overpassBuildingStock.peakDecade: 1900s-1910s (dense cluster of buildings from 1903-1915 visible in sample)
overpassBuildingStock.note: Highland Park's building stock is overwhelmingly pre-war, with a notable concentration of 1900-1915 construction. The neighborhood's 1994 Historic Preservation Overlay Zone (LA's largest) protected this stock from demolition.

neighborhoodContext.hpoz: Highland Park-Garvanza HPOZ established 1994 — LA's largest Historic Preservation Overlay Zone, protecting pre-war housing and commercial stock
neighborhoodContext.figueraStreet: Figueroa is the commercial spine. In the 1920s-30s it had 4 blocks of theaters. The trolley ran down it. Today: 14 sidewalk mosaics tell the story.
neighborhoodContext.1933Group: 1933 Group operates both La Cuevita and Highland Park Bowl — the bar group most responsible for Highland Park's nightlife identity. Named for the year Prohibition ended.
neighborhoodContext.chicanoCulture: Deep Chicano cultural infrastructure: Tenochtitlan mural, Judy Baca mural, Avenue 50 Studio (non-profit Chicano art gallery at 3714 N Figueroa), Self Help Graphics Dia de los Muertos exhibitions, La Cuevita's own Day of the Dead theming
